EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved across central Wyoming and brought heavy snow to much of northern and central Wyoming. The highest amount recorded was 24 inches at the Hogadon Basin Ski Area. In the lower elevations, the highest snowfall amounts occurred in the favored easterly to northeasterly upslope flow locations. Amounts around Lander ranged from 8 to 14 inches, with 6 to 12 inches in much of Johnson County and around Casper. The snow brought widespread transportation problems with numerous road closures at the height of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New snowfall amounts generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches, including 11 inches in Buffalo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An increasing pressure gradient ahead of an advancing winter storm and  strong jet streak moving over Wyoming brought high wind to much of central Wyoming. The strongest wind gusts were at Red Canyon along Highway 28 where gusts as high as 107 mph occurred. Wind gusts over 75 mph also occurred in the Lander foothills, the Absarokas and the Green Mountains. Wind gusts over 60 mph were fairly widespread across much of Natrona County as well as portions of northern Johnson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts over 60 mph occurred through much of Natrona County. Some of the notable gusts included 71 mph along Hat Six Road to the east of Casper and 68 mph at the Natrona County airport.

In the lower elevations, the highest snowfall amounts occurred in the favored easterly to northeasterly upslope flow locations. Amounts around Lander ranged from 8 to 14 inches, with 6 to 12 inches in much of Johnson County and around Casper. The snow brought widespread transportation problems with numerous road closures at the height of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Hogadon Ski area, 24 inches of new snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A group of four skiers unintentionally triggered an avalanche on a north-facing, sparsely treed slope above a steep-walled gully in the southern Absaroka Range, leading to one death and another person sustaining serious injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Summary from the USDA FS Bridger Teton Avalanche Center report titled Radio Tower Peak Avalanche Accident: On Saturday, January 4 2025, at approximately 11 am, a group of four skiers unintentionally triggered an avalanche on a north-facing, sparsely treed slope above a steep-walled gully approximately one mile from the parking pullout for Breccia Mountain. The avalanche was remotely triggered from below as the party attempted to cross a gully beneath a wind-loaded ridge. The triggered weak layer was formed during a cold, dry period early in the season. The weak layer was responsible for the region���s numerous human and snowmobile-triggered avalanches the week preceding this accident. This avalanche propagated 350��� uphill to a wind-loaded 38-40 degree slope, broken by cliff bands and across three distinct avalanche paths. The avalanche crown was 400��� across and ranged from one to four feet thick. The debris was a mixture of hard slab and softer surface snow, which slid 625 feet (400 vertical feet) into a terrain trap. The debris carried two members of the group, resulting in one serious injury and one fatality.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front brought gusty northwest winds and areas of light snow to much of northeast Wyoming. Periods of heavier upslope enhanced snowfall developed over the Wyoming Black Hills and Bear Lodge Mountains on the 22nd. Snowfall totals of 3 to 6 inches were common across these areas. The gusty winds produced blowing and drifting snow and some travel impacts across the area.

An arctic cold front passed through the region, bringing very cold temperatures from the 18th to the morning of the 21st. The coldest temperatures and lowest wind chills were during the overnight and morning hours of the 20th. Temperatures dropped to 10 below to 30 below zero in many areas, with northerly winds of 15 to over 25 mph producing wind chill values as low as 30 below to around 50 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continuation from January. An atmospheric river brought a prolonged snowfall across the western valleys and mountains. The was measured in feet in the higher elevations. Southwestern Yellowstone Park and the Tetons had the heaviest snowfall with as much as 40 inches of new snow reported. Over 2 feet of snow also fell in the Salt and Wyoming Range as well as the Wind River Range with a maximum 35 inches in the Absarokas. In the western valleys, amounts of over a foot fell in many locations. Meanwhile, East of the Divide, moisture overrunning the cold air and a strong jet stream brought heavy snow to the northern Cody Foothills, with over a foot of snow reported mainly from Cody and north and west. Mounts dropped sharply south of Cody. Meanwhile, other locations East of the Divide saw high wind. Wind gusts of over 100 mph were reported near Red Canyon with gusts to 86 mph in the Green Mountains and to around 70 mph around Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell along the western slopes of the Wind River Range. The highest amount of new snow was 27 inches at the Kendall Ranger Station SNOTEL site.

Wind gusts of over 100 mph were reported near Red Canyon with gusts to 86 mph in the Green Mountains and to around 70 mph around Casper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong atmospheric river moved over Wyoming and brought a prolonged snow that brought heavy snow to much of western and northern Wyoming. Favorable upslope flow in the Tetons resulted in very heavy snow with several reports over 2 feet of snow. The top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort received 55 inches of new snow. Northern portions of the Jackson valley picked up significant snow, but lesser amounts fell in southern portions where there was some mixing with rain. The Salt and Wyoming Range saw as much as 40 inches of new snow, with the nearby Star Valley receiving anywhere from 10 to 16 inches of news now. Over 20 inches of new snow fell in Yellowstone Park and the Absarokas as well. East of the Divide, heavy snow was limited to areas north of a Cody to Greybull line, with almost 11 inches falling to the west of Cody.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in southwestern portions of Yellowstone Park, with a maximum of 20 inches at the Lewis Lake Divide SNOTEL site.

Natrona County had the strongest wind gusts in the lower elevations, with gusts to 70 mph at the Natrona County Airport to the west of Casper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continuation from January. An atmospheric river brought a prolonged snowfall across the western valleys and mountains. The was measured in feet in the higher elevations. Southwestern Yellowstone Park and the Tetons had the heaviest snowfall with as much as 40 inches of new snow reported. Over 2 feet of snow also fell in the Salt and Wyoming Range as well as the Wind River Range with a maximum 35 inches in the Absarokas. In the western valleys, amounts of over a foot fell in many locations. Meanwhile, East of the Divide, moisture overrunning the cold air and a strong jet stream brought heavy snow to the northern Cody Foothills, with over a foot of snow reported mainly from Cody and north and west. Mounts dropped sharply south of Cody. Meanwhile, other locations East of the Divide saw high wind. Wind gusts of over 100 mph were reported near Red Canyon with gusts to 86 mph in the Green Mountains and to around 70 mph around Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow in southern Yellowstone Park. The highest amount reported was 40 inches the the Lewis Lake SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level height falls ahead of an approaching trough resulted in the development of scattered convection throughout the day. Given dry low levels and a well mixed boundary layer, DCAPE values were high, and multiple dry to mostly dry microbursts were observed across southwest Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Evanston North mesonet station recorded a wind gust of 59 mph as a result of outflow winds associated with a nearby cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level height falls ahead of an approaching trough resulted in the development of scattered convection throughout the day. Given dry low levels and a well mixed boundary layer, DCAPE values were high, and multiple dry to mostly dry microbursts were observed across southwest Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Evanston North mesonet station recorded an initial strong wind gust of 58 mph at 2:30 pm MST, followed by a subsequent peak wind gust of 62 mph at 3:00 pm MST, and a final strong wind gust of 58 mph at 3:20 pm MST. More modest wind gusts in the 50 mph to 57 mph were recorded in between these observations. These wind gusts were the result of outflow winds associated with a nearby cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered convection in an environment characterized by fairly dry subcloud layers and associated high DCAPE values resulted in isolated microburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Muddy Creek mesonet station recorded a wind gust of 66 mph as a result of outflow winds associated with a nearby cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved across Wyoming and brought severe thunderstorms to portions of western and central Wyoming. Wind gusts over 58 mph occurred in Lincoln, Fremont, Sublette, Natrona and Sweetwater Counties with a maximum gust of 71 mph west of Daniel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms moving across southeast Wyoming and western Nebraska during the afternoon and evening hours produced gusty conditions and hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At least 4 debris flows within the Elk Burn Scar occurred along US-14 that closed the road for 3-4 hours. Also caused a log jam along the Little Tongue Canyon on Little Tongue Ranch, which caused localized flooding and damage to a barn. 0.7 inches of rain fell from 7 to 7:30pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of shortwaves moved across central Wyoming and brought a round of severe thunderstorms to central Wyoming. Wind gusts past 58 mph were reported in Fremont, Natrona, Big Horn and Washakie Counties, with a maximum reported gust of 76 mph at the town of Natrona in western Natrona County.  At Worland, some trees were blown down, bringing scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 67 mph wind gust was reported at the Southern Big Horn County airport near Greybull.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms occurred for a second day in a row. In Sublette County, a trained spotter reported golf ball size hail north of Cora. A second round of severe thunderstorms occurred in the evening, with wind gusts of 66 mph at the Rock Springs aiport and gusts as high as 61 mph near Casper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of high based thunderstorms produced high wind gusts across Sweetwater County. The maximum wind gusts of 61 mph were reported at the Rock Springs Airport and along Interstate 80 to the east of Wamsutter.

An upper level low moved across Wyoming and brought a round of strong to severe thunderstorms to western and central Wyoming. The severe thunderstorms brought both hail and strong wind. The maximum wind gust reported was 75 mph near Muddy Gap with wind gusts over 58 mph also reported in Sublette, Sweetwater, Lincoln and Hot Springs Counties. In Afton, trees were blown onto power lines leading a power outage. A pile of loose rocks was blown down near Tabletop Mountain in Johnson County. In Fremont County, a supercell thunderstorm moved southeast dropping penny to ping pong size hail, with the largest hail reported to the northwest of Lander.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 mph wind gust was reported at the Dubois airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado impacted a home and adjacent homestead off of Grey Rd in far western Crook county. The tornado produced EF0 damage to several outbuildings south of the main single family residence. EF0 consisted of pushing and destroying a small shed outbuilding and pushing a storage silo off its pad and rotating it 45 degrees to the SW from its origin. The tornado tracked NE and continued to produce EF0 damage near the main home. Several trees near the home were snapped and the home loss some roofing material. The tornado continued to the NE before shifting to the ENE where it intensified to EF1. The tornado damaged a large outbuilding, ripping two concrete foots out of the ground and pushing the entire concrete brace from the foundation some 10 yards to the ENE. The entire structure had slid off the foundation with the structures walls all collapsed. The tornado then entered open field with no damage indicators available. A large debris field of tree branches and building material was thrown to the E, NE and N of the tornado track.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Survey team determined that an EF-1 landspout tornado  impacted a home and adjacent homestead 10 NW of  Moorcroft.

The Vees Fire was reported on Saturday, July 26, 2025 south of Ten Sleep, Wyoming in Washakie County. Fire growth was halted on July 31st by heavy rain in the region. The fire ultimately burned 5,229 acres on a combination of Bureau of Land Management and private lands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vees Fire was reported on Saturday, July 26, 2025 south of Ten Sleep, Wyoming in Washakie County. Fire growth was halted on July 31st by heavy rain in the region. The fire ultimately burned 5,229 acres on a combination of Bureau of Land Management and private lands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening of July 30, 2025, numerous thunderstorms developed over the Moneta area to the east of Shoshoni, WY and to the north of Highway 20/26. Radar derived rainfall estimates between 7 PM and 11 PM MDT were 2 to 3 inches, with an additional 1 inch of rainfall estimated the day before (July 29). This caused the highway culverts for Frenchie Draw to be overwhelmed. WYDOT employees at the time observed water 4 ft deep flowing across Highway 20/26 near Frenchie Draw, prompting a closure of the highway for approximately 2 hours, after which time water receded. WYDOT made repairs in Sep 2025 to backfill areas of washout along the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WYDOT employee reported water was 4 feet deep flowing across the highway near Frenchie Draw. Water was on road for about 2 hours, which was the length of the closure. Damages estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific trough moved into western Wyoming and brought heavy snow to the western mountains. The heaviest snow fell in Yellowstone Park where the Two Oceans Plateau SNOTEL site reported 28 inches of new snow. The Absarokas, Tetons, Wind River, Salt River, and Wyoming Ranges all reported new snow totals of over 20 inches. The heavy snow resulted in difficult travel conditions and road closures. In areas east of the Continental Divide, high wind was the main hazard. The most widespread high wind occurred in Natrona County where there were widespread wind gusts over 70 mph, with a maximum wind gust of 88 mph along Hat Six Road to the east of Casper. The Johnson County airport had a wind gust of 81 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the Eastern Wind River Range, with a maximum amount of 16 inches at the Deer Park SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent ridging across the northern Rockies brought record warmth to western and central Wyoming for December. Many airports with a 20 to 30 or more year period of record had the warmest December on record. Most sites were at least 10 degrees above normal for the month, with Riverton running more than 15 degrees above normal. Numerous high temperature records were set throughout the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: It was a record warm December at the Greybull airport. The average temperature of 32.8 degrees beat the old record of 29.0 degrees set last year. The period of record dates back to 1998.

The Red Canyon fire started on the northern foothills of the Bridger Mountains in Hot Springs County on the afternoon of August 13th due to a lightning strike. The fire had grown to 3,000 acres by August 14th. A combination of hot and dry conditions as well as gusty wind caused the fire to exhibit extreme fire behavior and spread rapidly, especially on August 16th when several evacuations were ordered to the southeast of Thermopolis and fire spread from Hot Springs county into northern Fremont County. The fire had grown to over 100,000 acres by August 20th as the hot and dry conditions continued. Containment increased starting on August 22nd as cooler weather and higher humidity moved in. The fire was 80 percent contained by August 27th, and all evacuations and road closures were lifted. All in all, time Red Canyon fire burned nearly 125,000 acres.

The Dollar Lake was discovered on the afternoon of August 21st in the western Wind River Mountains. The fire had grown to over 15,000 acres by August 31st, and several forest service access roads were closed. This fire continued into September. Please see the September Storm Events Database entry for a complete summary of this fire.

Wyoming is often windy, but this was an expectational wind event. A notably strong 180 knot jet stream crossed Wyoming and brought widespread high wind statewide. There were numerous reports of wind gusts over 100 mph in the mountains, including a 110 mph gust along the Chief Joseph Highway in the Absaroka Range, a 123 mph gust in Red Canyon near South Pass, and a 144 mph gust at Mount Coffin in the Wyoming Range. This is the second highest gust recorded in Wyoming history. In the lower elevations, some notable wind gusts at official reporting stations included 92 mph at the Lander airport, 78 mph at both the Casper and Rock Springs airports, and 71 mph at the Buffalo airport. In the mountains, wind combined with snow to bring whiteout conditions to mountain passes, resulting in several highway closures. The strong wind in Lander brought down power lines and resulted in a power outage to around 2500 customers for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the Wind River Mountains, with 17 inches at the Deer Park SNOTEL site. There were multiple reports of wind gusts past 60 mph.

The Sleep Ranch fire started on August 14th to the east of Meeteetsee and grew to over 20,000 acres by August 16th.  The fire was 90 percent contained by August 19th, burning a total of almost 21,000 acres.

A significant push of monsoonal moisture brought rainfall to much of Wyoming on 8/27/25. Thunderstorms with high rainfall rates near Jeffrey City led to flash flooding for portions of the town. The public reported ponding water in low spots throughout Jeffrey City. Radar estimates showed 1.0 to 1.5 inches of rain falling on the town of Jeffrey City within a 1 hour period, with up to 2 inches falling west of town. Rainfall rates were estimated to be as high as 6 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported ponding water in low spots throughout Jeffrey City. Radar estimates showed 1.0 to 1.5 inches of rain falling on the town of Jeffrey City within a 1 hour period, with up to 2 inches falling west of town. Rainfall rates were estimated to be as high as 6 inches per hour.

In the lower elevations, some notable wind gusts at official reporting stations included 92 mph at the Lander airport, 78 mph at both the Casper and Rock Springs airports, and 71 mph at the Buffalo airport. In the mountains, wind combined with snow to bring whiteout conditions to mountain passes, resulting in several highway closures. The strong wind in Lander brought down power lines and resulted in a power outage to around 2500 customers for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Split Creek RAWS site reported a 76 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy mountain snow and strong winds in the latter half of December led to multiple avalanches of note in western Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WYDOT scheduled an avalanche control mission in the early hours of Dec 22 in response to recent weather activity on Wyoming State Highway 22 Teton Pass. Crews remotely detonated avalanche infrastructure in the area known as Glory Bowl and brought down the largest controlled avalanche seen by crews in recent years. A snow slide over 30 feet high and 250 feet wide buried both lanes of travel at milepost 10 on the closed road. Clean up operations lasted about 14 hours and the road was reopened just after 5 PM on Dec 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific weather system moved into Wyoming and brought heavy snow to the western mountains and valleys. The heaviest snow fell in the Tetons with 22 inches of new snow at the Jackson Hole Mountain Resort. Over a foot of new snow also fell in portions of Yellowstone Park, the Absarokas, and the western Bighorn Range. For the valleys, up to 14 inches fell in Star Valley and 11 inches in Jackson Hole.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the Star Valley, including 14 inches southeast of Smoot and 10 inches near Thayne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific trough moved into western Wyoming and brought heavy snow to the western mountains. The heaviest snow fell in Yellowstone Park where the Two Oceans Plateau SNOTEL site reported 28 inches of new snow. The Absarokas, Tetons, Wind River, Salt River, and Wyoming Ranges all reported new snow totals of over 20 inches. The heavy snow resulted in difficult travel conditions and road closures. In areas east of the Continental Divide, high wind was the main hazard. The most widespread high wind occurred in Natrona County where there were widespread wind gusts over 70 mph, with a maximum wind gust of 88 mph along Hat Six Road to the east of Casper. The Johnson County airport had a wind gust of 81 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in Yellowstone Park. The highest amounts was at the Two Oceans Plateau SNOTEL site where 28 inches of new snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy mountain snow and strong winds in the latter half of December led to multiple avalanches of note in western Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excerpt from the Bridger-Teton Avalanche Center Accident Report: On Saturday, December 20, 2025 at approximately 2 PM, one skier in a party of two unintentionally triggered an avalanche on a north-facing, steep, rocky, and sparsely treed chute known as The Claw approximately two miles south of the main parking lot at Teton Pass. The slope failed above the skier. The debris ran 1,000 feet (600��� vertical fall) to the base of the avalanche path, carrying and critically burying the first skier among trees. The|skier was taken to the local hospital and later flown to a larger regional medical center where he died six days later of multi-systems trauma sustained in the avalanche.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific trough moved into western Wyoming and brought heavy snow to the western mountains. The heaviest snow fell in Yellowstone Park where the Two Oceans Plateau SNOTEL site reported 28 inches of new snow. The Absarokas, Tetons, Wind River, Salt River, and Wyoming Ranges all reported new snow totals of over 20 inches. The heavy snow resulted in difficult travel conditions and road closures. In areas east of the Continental Divide, high wind was the main hazard. The most widespread high wind occurred in Natrona County where there were widespread wind gusts over 70 mph, with a maximum wind gust of 88 mph along Hat Six Road to the east of Casper. The Johnson County airport had a wind gust of 81 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind was widespread across much of Natrona County, with gusts over 65 mph reported in many locations. Some of the highest gusts included 88 mph along Hat Six Road to the east of Casper, 86 mph at Scenic Drive on the south side of Casper and 76 mph at the Natrona County airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A longwave upper-level trough was positioned over the Intermountain West upstream of an amplified ridge that had its axis over the Great Plains. Southerly flow ahead of the trough brought anomalously high atmospheric moisture to western Wyoming, sourced from both the Pacific Ocean and Gulf of Mexico. CAPE values in the Alta, WY area were in the 200 to 400 J/kg range with 0-6 km shear in the 50 to 60 kt range. In the 30 minutes prior to producing this tornado, the parent thunderstorm moved across the Teton Basin between Driggs, ID and Victor, ID producing pea-sized to nickel-sized hail. One strong thunderstorm embedded in a line of storms produced an EF1 tornado near Alta, Wyoming in the Caribou-Targhee National Forest. Several trees had to be cut and cleared from forest service access roads.||This is the 3rd confirmed tornado in Teton County history (1987 and 2022 each had 1 as well). It is also the latest tornado (warm-season-wise, and calendar-year-wise) recorded in Wyoming history, with only 1 other recorded tornado in October (10/7/1969 in Goshen County). As of Dec 2025, November through March have no reported tornados in Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On 10/15/25 from 1:35 to 1:37 PM MDT, a thunderstorm produced an EF1 tornado near Alta, WY in the Teton Canyon portion of the Caribou-Targhee National Forest. Reports from social media after the event mentioned tree damage in the Teton Canyon area. Later drone footage taken by the local sheriff's office during the week after this event, as well as satellite imagery, identified a swath of tree damage consistent with 87-104 mph winds (EF1). ||Wind speed was determined using the Enhanced Fujita Scale Damage Indicator for #28 ���Tree - Softwood���, since this tornado occurred in a dense coniferous portion of the Caribou-Targhee National Forest. No structures were in the path. Aerial drone photography showed hundreds of trees blown down by the tornado, with a clear damage swath/path through the forest. A majority of trees in the path were uprooted (Degree of Damage 3, 87 mph) or had their trunks snapped (DoD 4, 104 mph). Bark was visible on trees, and there was no notable debarking or stripping of branches seen in imagery (DoD 5). Given relatively healthy trees with no reason to believe trees were stronger or weaker than average, the lower and upper bounds for the DoDs were not utilized, and expected values were referenced. This led to a wind speed estimate of 87 to 104 mph, which is rated EF1 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale.||The tornado damage path is approximately 1.0 miles long, with a maximum width of 125 yards. The tornado damage path started at an elevation of 7900 ft ASL and ended around 6900 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Wyoming and Nebraska December 17th. Scattered snow squalls were observed along the Interstate 80 and Interstate 25 corridor, some producing visibilities below one quarter mile, gusts over 60 MPH, and flash freezing on Interstates. Some of the strongest winds observed across central and southeast Wyoming occurred during this event with a peak gust of 112 MPH reported along Interstate 25. There was also a brief fire south of Cheyenne late in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All wind sensors in the zone reported very strong wind gusts over 70 MPH. Peak wind gust of 112 MPH was measured on a handheld kestrel by a well-know trained weather spotter just west of Interstate 25, southwest of Glendo. 3 miles to the east, a peak gust was recorded at the WYDOT site in Coleman, which measured a gust of 95 MPH. Webcam images show a blown over or flipped 16 wheeler truck in the middle of Interstate 25 south bound. In addition, photos provided by the public show wind damage in and around Wheatland Wyoming with a roof ripped off an unknown nearby structure. On a separate property, a shed was ripped apart with the siding stuck in some nearby trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low moved across Wyoming and brought a round of strong to severe thunderstorms to western and central Wyoming. The severe thunderstorms brought both hail and strong wind. The maximum wind gust reported was 75 mph near Muddy Gap with wind gusts over 58 mph also reported in Sublette, Sweetwater, Lincoln and Hot Springs Counties. In Afton, trees were blown onto power lines leading a power outage. A pile of loose rocks was blown down near Tabletop Mountain in Johnson County. In Fremont County, a supercell thunderstorm moved southeast dropping penny to ping pong size hail, with the largest hail reported to the northwest of Lander.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sheephearders monument was blown down near Tabletop Mountain in the Bighorn Mountain Range. Wind measurements from nearby sensors and structure damage support a 60 to 75 mph wind speed estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Willow Creek fire continued to burn in the Bridger-Teton National Forest at the beginning of the month. Increasing monsoonal moisture and rising humidity enabled fire crews to increase containment on the fire after Labor Day, with containment increasing to 73 percent by September 7th, with near full containment by September 16th. In total, the fire burned 4066 acres and resulted in closure of several Forest Service Roads and some temporary evacuations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Wyoming and Nebraska December 17th. Scattered snow squalls were observed along the Interstate 80 and Interstate 25 corridor, some producing visibilities below one quarter mile, gusts over 60 MPH, and flash freezing on Interstates. There was also a brief fire south of Cheyenne late in the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 17, 2025, the entire region experienced a long-duration, high-impact wind event resulting in widespread damage and impacts. While strong winds are a normal occurrence across this region, this event stands out due to its spatial extent and prolonged period of impact, with many locations experiencing high sustained winds and wind gusts for 6 or more hours. Damage reported as a result of this weather system includes numerous, downed trees and power lines. Widespread severe driving conditions were reported with multiple road closures as a result of blown-over semis and trailers and fallen debris. Numerous weather observation stations across the reservation recorded wind gusts over 50mph for several hours. Strong winds lingered into the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated due to adjacent Pryor Mountains gusting to 79 mph at Wild Horse raws.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More strong winds occurred across the area, producing gusts up to 85 mph (mainly 55-65 mph elsewhere).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power outages occurred in Story, WY due to strong winds. Adjacent weather stations reported gusts in the 60s to low 70s.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Natrona County Emergency Manager reported 2.25 inch hail to the northwest of Mills.

The Dollar Lake Fire continued to burn in the western Wind River Mountains in September, growing to over 19,000 acres by September 5th. Following that, a period of cooler and wetter weather slowed the spread of the fire. The fire was largely contained by September 24th, burning a total of 19,467 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed over the Wind River Mountain and moved toward Lander, producing strong wind gusts. The highest wind gust reported was 78 mph in the higher elevations to the southwest of Lander. The Lander airport had a maximum wind gust of 69 mph.
